news_summary: Recent advances reveal how ketamine rapidly alleviates severe depression, highlight a simple diet change to improve gut health, identify parental stress as a factor in childhood obesity, and introduce monthly HIV-drug injections as an effective alternative to daily pills.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes révèlent comment la kétamine soulage rapidement la dépression sévère, mettent en évidence un simple changement alimentaire pour améliorer la santé intestinale, identifient le stress parental comme un facteur d'obésité infantile et introduisent des injections mensuelles de médicaments contre le VIH comme alternative efficace aux comprimés quotidiens.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances revelan cómo la ketamina alivia rápidamente la depresión grave, destacan un sencillo cambio en la dieta para mejorar la salud intestinal, identifican el estrés parental como un factor en la obesidad infantil e introducen las inyecciones mensuales de medicamentos contra el VIH como una alternativa eficaz a las pastillas diarias.

articles.title: Brain scans reveal how ketamine quickly lifts severe depression
articles.summary: A new brain-imaging study has revealed how ketamine produces its fast antidepressant effects in people with treatment-resistant depression. Researchers tracked changes in a critical brain receptor that helps neurons communicate and found that ketamine reshapes its activity in specific brain regions tied to mood and reward. These shifts strongly matched improvements in patients’ symptoms. The findings could help scientists develop better ways to predict who will benefit from ketamine therapy.
articles.content:
Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a major global health problem and one of the leading causes of disability. About 30% of people diagnosed with depression develop treatment-resistant depression (TRD), meaning their symptoms do not improve sufficiently with standard antidepressant medications. Ketamine has gained attention as a fast-acting antidepressant for people with TRD. However, scientists have not fully understood how it works inside the human brain, which has made it difficult to refine and personalize this treatment.

A new study published in Molecular Psychiatry on March 5, 2026, sought to clarify this mystery. The research was led by Professor Takuya Takahashi of the Department of Physiology at Yokohama City University Graduate School of Medicine in Japan. The team used an advanced positron emission tomography (PET) imaging method to directly observe changes in glutamate α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazole propionic acid receptor (AMPAR). This receptor is a key protein that helps regulate communication between brain cells and plays an important role in synaptic plasticity and glutamatergic signaling in patients receiving ketamine.

Prof. Takahashi explained, "Although ketamine has shown rapid antidepressant effects in patients with treatment-resistant depression, its molecular mechanism in the human brain has remained unclear."

Visualizing Brain Receptors With a Novel PET Tracer

The research relied on a PET tracer developed earlier by the team, known as [¹¹C]K-2. This tracer allows scientists to visualize cell-surface AMPAR directly in the living human brain. Previous laboratory and animal studies suggested that ketamine's antidepressant effects involve AMPAR activity. The new research provides the first direct evidence of this process occurring in humans.

To conduct the study, the researchers combined data from three registered clinical trials carried out in Japan. The study group included 34 patients diagnosed with TRD and 49 healthy participants who served as controls.

Patients received intravenous ketamine or a placebo over a two-week period. PET brain imaging was performed before the start of treatment and again after the final infusion. This approach allowed researchers to compare changes in AMPAR levels and distribution in the brain over time.

Region-Specific Brain Changes Linked to Symptom Relief

The results showed that people with TRD had widespread abnormalities in AMPAR density compared with healthy participants. These differences appeared in specific brain regions rather than across the brain as a whole.

Ketamine did not produce uniform changes throughout the brain. Instead, improvements in depressive symptoms were linked to dynamic, region-specific adjustments in AMPAR levels. Some cortical areas showed increased receptor density, while reductions were seen in regions associated with reward processing, especially the habenula. These region-specific shifts were strongly connected to improvements in patients' depressive symptoms.

"Ketamine's antidepressant effect in patients with TRD is mediated by dynamic changes in AMPAR in the living human brain," Prof. Takahashi explained. "Using a novel PET tracer, [11C]K-2, we were able to visualize how ketamine alters AMPAR distribution across specific brain regions and how these changes correlate with improvements in depressive symptoms."

These observations provide direct human evidence that supports mechanisms previously identified in animal studies and connects them to real clinical antidepressant effects.

Potential Biomarker for Predicting Treatment Response

The findings do more than clarify how ketamine works. They may also have practical clinical value. PET imaging of AMPAR could potentially serve as a biomarker that helps doctors evaluate and predict how individuals with TRD will respond to ketamine treatment.

Because many patients do not respond to standard antidepressants, identifying reliable biological markers for treatment response remains an important goal in mental health care.

Toward More Personalized Depression Treatments

By allowing scientists to directly observe AMPAR activity in the living human brain, this research helps bridge a long-standing gap between laboratory research and clinical psychiatry. The results identify AMPAR modulation as a central mechanism behind ketamine's rapid antidepressant effects and suggest that AMPAR PET imaging could guide more personalized treatment strategies in the future.

Ultimately, this work could support the development of more precise therapies for people living with treatment-resistant depression.

This work was supported by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (Special Coordination Funds for Promoting Science and Technology); the Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development (AMED) (grant numbers: JP18dm0207023, JP19dm0207072, JP24wm0625304, JP25gm7010019, and JP20dm0107124); the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science KAKENHI (grant numbers: 22H03001, 20H00549, 20H05922, 23K10432, 19H03587, 20K20603, 22K15793, and 21K07508); the Takeda Science Foundation; the Keio Next-Generation Research Project Program; the SENSHIN Medical Research Foundation; and the Japan Research Foundation for Clinical Pharmacology.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding how ketamine rapidly alleviates treatment-resistant depression by visualizing brain receptor changes in humans. This discovery has broad implications for improving mental health treatment worldwide, offering hope for personalized therapies for a major global health issue.
articles.personality_title: Brain scans show how ketamine quickly eases severe depression
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Depression affects millions worldwide, and about 30% of people do not get better with regular medicines. This hard-to-treat form is called treatment-resistant depression (TRD). Ketamine is a drug known to help some people with TRD very quickly, but scientists didn't fully understand how it works in the brain.

**What happened** – Researchers in Japan used a special brain scan called PET imaging to watch changes in a brain receptor named AMPAR, which helps brain cells communicate. They studied 34 patients with TRD and 49 healthy people. Patients received ketamine or a placebo over two weeks. Scans before and after treatment showed that ketamine changed AMPAR activity in certain brain areas linked to mood and reward, and these changes matched patients feeling better.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have directly seen how ketamine affects human brains at a molecular level. The study helps explain why ketamine works so fast to improve depression symptoms. It also points to AMPAR activity as a possible marker doctors can use to predict who will benefit from ketamine, making treatment more personalized.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to use this brain imaging method to guide treatment plans for people with TRD. Future work may develop better drugs or tests based on AMPAR activity, helping more patients find relief faster.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists used brain scans to reveal how ketamine quickly changes specific brain receptors, helping people with hard-to-treat depression feel better.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scans cérébraux révèlent comment la kétamine soulage rapidement la dépression sévère
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La dépression touche des millions de personnes dans le monde, et environ 30 % ne s'améliorent pas avec les médicaments habituels. Cette forme difficile à traiter s'appelle la dépression résistante au traitement (DRT). La kétamine est un médicament connu pour aider rapidement certaines personnes atteintes de DRT, mais son fonctionnement dans le cerveau n'était pas encore clair.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs japonais ont utilisé une technique spéciale d'imagerie cérébrale appelée PET pour observer les changements d'un récepteur cérébral nommé AMPAR, qui aide les cellules du cerveau à communiquer. Ils ont étudié 34 patients avec DRT et 49 personnes en bonne santé. Les patients ont reçu de la kétamine ou un placebo pendant deux semaines. Les scans avant et après le traitement ont montré que la kétamine modifiait l'activité de l'AMPAR dans certaines zones du cerveau liées à l'humeur et à la récompense, et ces changements correspondaient à une amélioration des symptômes.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois que des scientifiques observent directement comment la kétamine agit au niveau moléculaire dans le cerveau humain. Cette étude aide à comprendre pourquoi la kétamine agit si rapidement pour améliorer la dépression. Elle montre aussi que l'activité de l'AMPAR pourrait servir de marqueur pour prédire qui bénéficiera de la kétamine, rendant le traitement plus personnalisé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent utiliser cette méthode d'imagerie pour guider les traitements des personnes atteintes de DRT. À l'avenir, cela pourrait permettre de développer de meilleurs médicaments ou tests basés sur l'activité de l'AMPAR, aidant plus de patients à se sentir mieux rapidement.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scans cérébraux ont révélé comment la kétamine modifie rapidement des récepteurs spécifiques, aidant les personnes souffrant de dépression résistante à se sentir mieux.
articles.personality_title_es: Escáneres cerebrales muestran cómo la ketamina alivia rápidamente la depresión severa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La depresión afecta a millones de personas en todo el mundo, y alrededor del 30 % no mejora con los medicamentos comunes. Esta forma difícil de tratar se llama depresión resistente al tratamiento (DRT). La ketamina es un medicamento que ayuda rápidamente a algunas personas con DRT, pero no se entendía bien cómo funciona en el cerebro.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Japón usaron una técnica especial llamada imagen por PET para observar cambios en un receptor cerebral llamado AMPAR, que ayuda a las células del cerebro a comunicarse. Estudiaron a 34 pacientes con DRT y 49 personas sanas. Los pacientes recibieron ketamina o un placebo durante dos semanas. Los escáneres antes y después mostraron que la ketamina cambió la actividad del AMPAR en áreas del cerebro relacionadas con el estado de ánimo y la recompensa, y estos cambios coincidieron con la mejoría de los pacientes.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven directamente cómo la ketamina afecta el cerebro humano a nivel molecular. El estudio ayuda a explicar por qué la ketamina mejora los síntomas de la depresión tan rápido. También sugiere que la actividad del AMPAR podría usarse para predecir quién se beneficiará de la ketamina, haciendo el tratamiento más personalizado.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan usar esta técnica de imagen cerebral para guiar los tratamientos de personas con DRT. En el futuro, esto podría ayudar a crear mejores medicamentos o pruebas basadas en la actividad del AMPAR, ayudando a más pacientes a mejorar rápidamente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos usaron escáneres cerebrales para mostrar cómo la ketamina cambia rápido receptores específicos, ayudando a personas con depresión difícil de tratar a sentirse mejor.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a human brain silhouette filled with glowing, interconnected neural pathways highlighted by clusters of small, radiant receptor-like nodes shifting in vibrant patterns, set against a soft, natural-toned background symbolizing healing and dynamic balance.

articles.title: Scientists say this simple diet change could transform your gut health
articles.summary: A growing trend called “fibermaxxing” is putting dietary fiber in the spotlight—and for good reason. Fiber plays a powerful role in keeping the body healthy, from supporting digestion and feeding beneficial gut microbes to helping regulate blood sugar and cholesterol. Researchers say getting enough fiber may even lower the risk of conditions like obesity, diabetes, and certain cancers.
articles.content:
How much fiber you eat can strongly influence your overall health. Fiber supports healthy digestion and has been linked to a lower risk of certain cancers, among many other benefits. That growing awareness may help explain why the "fibermaxxing" trend is gaining attention.

Fibermaxxing refers to consuming at least the recommended daily amount of fiber for your body weight each day. The idea has gained traction across social media and traditional media this year.

Jennifer Lee is a scientist at the Jean Mayer USDA Human Nutrition Research Center on Aging at Tufts University. Her research focuses on how shifts in gut health and differences between sexes affect metabolism throughout a person's life.

Lee says she is not surprised that fibermaxxing has become popular. In fact, she sees it as a sign that more people are recognizing an important distinction between lifespan and healthspan. Living longer does not necessarily mean living those years in good health, so many people are searching for ways to stay healthier as they age.

"There is a nine-year gap between living to a certain age in good health and then living in poor quality of health at the end of your life," Lee added. "Behavioral or nutritional strategies that can keep someone healthy are very on trend right now."

Research shows that consistently low fiber intake can contribute to metabolic and cardiovascular problems, including diabetes and obesity.

"If you're not consuming a lot of fiber, you're possibly consuming calories from other macronutrient groups, and they may be high in carbohydrates or fats, which can lead to weight gain," Lee said. "Then, depending on a number of factors that may impact one's cancer risk, a fiber deficiency may increase your risk for certain cancers, such as colorectal, breast, and prostate cancer."

Overall, Lee explained that adding more fiber to your daily diet tends to produce wide ranging health benefits.

How Much Fiber Do I Need? You can find a detailed recommendation for your personal nutritional intake via the USDA's National Agriculture Library Dietary Reference Intakes (DRI) calculator.

Meeting Daily Fiber Intake Recommendations

According to the Dietary Guidelines for Americans, 2020-2025, published by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) and United States Department of Health and Human Services, adults should consume between 22 and 34 grams of fiber each day, depending on age and sex.

Lee also pointed to a simple guideline. For every 1,000 calories consumed, people should aim for about 14 grams of fiber. As people get older and typically eat fewer calories, their recommended fiber intake decreases accordingly.

"For someone between 19 and 30 years old, a female's average recommended daily fiber intake would be 28 grams, based on a 2,000-calorie diet," Lee said. "But for a male in that same age range, the recommended amount of fiber increases to 34 grams because they're eating a little bit more."

Soluble vs. Insoluble Fiber

Lee noted that dietary fiber falls into two main categories. Soluble fiber dissolves in water and slows digestion, while insoluble fiber helps move waste through the digestive tract.

"Soluble fiber attracts water into your gut and forms a gel-like substance," Lee said. "It keeps you full, helps you feel satiated, and once it makes it into the colon, can provide or serve as a substrate for microbiota, meaning your microbiota can metabolize the food that you digest as well. So, this type of fiber serves as a beneficial food source for the microbes."

Soluble fiber can also help regulate blood sugar by slowing digestion and reducing sudden spikes in glucose levels. It may also help lower cholesterol by preventing some cholesterol from being absorbed into the bloodstream.

Foods rich in soluble fiber include many fruits and vegetables, such as apples, avocados, bananas, cabbage, broccoli, and cauliflower. Legumes, beans, and oatmeal are also good sources. Insoluble fiber is commonly found in whole grains, nuts, and seeds.

"Insoluble fiber, on the other hand, cannot be dissolved and will not contribute to the calories you consume," Lee said. "The body can't take up energy from insoluble fiber, but it is critical to consume because it's the bulk of substrate that helps you have a bowel movement. Because insoluble fiber bulks up your stool, it helps to prevent constipation."

To maintain a healthy balance, Lee recommends consuming roughly twice as much insoluble fiber as soluble fiber each day. For example, if your daily goal is 30 grams of fiber, about 20 grams should come from insoluble fiber and 10 grams from soluble fiber.

How Can I Eat More Fiber? The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention put together a resource on how fiber can help to manage diabetes, which includes tips for adding more fiber to your diet by eating things like fiber-friendly breakfasts.

Fiber Supplements and Potential Side Effects

For people who struggle to get enough fiber through food alone, supplements may help fill the gap. Lee noted that many adults fall short of recommended fiber intake levels, making supplementation a practical option in some cases.

"The majority of adults are not meeting their dietary fiber intake levels, so generally supplementation is a good strategy to meet recommended levels."

Fiber supplements are available as capsules or powders that can be mixed into drinks. However, Lee cautioned that increasing fiber intake too quickly can cause digestive issues while the body adjusts.

"You could run into the extremes of eating too much, where if you're not drinking enough water to hydrate and exceed the amount of soluble and insoluble fiber, you can get constipated," Lee said. "The other extreme is that some people respond differently to fiber and they run the risk of getting diarrhea. You really should check in with your body, since you know how your body is responding to what you're challenging it with daily."
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a scientifically supported dietary change—'fibermaxxing'—that can significantly improve gut health and reduce risks of obesity, diabetes, and certain cancers. It provides detailed, actionable information with broad public health implications, promoting a positive, accessible lifestyle change.
articles.personality_title: Eating more fiber daily can improve your gut health and lower disease risks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Eating enough fiber is important for good digestion and overall health. Fiber helps feed good bacteria in the gut and can lower the chance of problems like obesity, diabetes, and some cancers. Recently, a trend called “fibermaxxing” has encouraged people to eat the recommended amount of fiber every day.

**What happened** – Scientists like Jennifer Lee from Tufts University explain that fibermaxxing means eating enough fiber based on your body weight. Adults should eat between 22 and 34 grams of fiber daily, depending on age and sex. Fiber comes in two types: soluble fiber, which dissolves in water and helps control blood sugar and cholesterol, and insoluble fiber, which helps waste move through the digestive system. Foods like fruits, vegetables, whole grains, beans, nuts, and seeds are good sources.

**Impact** – Getting enough fiber helps keep the gut healthy, supports digestion, and lowers risks for diseases such as diabetes, obesity, and certain cancers like colorectal cancer. Many adults don’t eat enough fiber, so this information can help people make better food choices. Fiber supplements can also help, but it’s important to increase fiber slowly to avoid stomach problems.

**What's next step** – People can use tools like the USDA’s Dietary Reference Intakes calculator to find out how much fiber they need. Adding more fiber-rich foods to daily meals or using supplements can help meet these goals. Scientists will continue studying how fiber affects health over time, possibly leading to new advice for preventing disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eating the right amount of fiber every day supports gut health and lowers the risk of serious diseases like diabetes and certain cancers.
articles.personality_title_fr: Manger plus de fibres chaque jour améliore la santé intestinale et réduit les risques de maladie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Manger suffisamment de fibres est important pour une bonne digestion et la santé générale. Les fibres nourrissent les bonnes bactéries de l’intestin et peuvent réduire les risques d’obésité, de diabète et de certains cancers. Récemment, une tendance appelée « fibermaxxing » encourage à consommer la quantité recommandée de fibres chaque jour.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques comme Jennifer Lee de l’Université Tufts expliquent que le fibermaxxing consiste à manger assez de fibres selon son poids corporel. Les adultes devraient consommer entre 22 et 34 grammes de fibres par jour, selon l’âge et le sexe. Les fibres se divisent en deux types : solubles, qui se dissolvent dans l’eau et aident à contrôler le sucre et le cholestérol, et insolubles, qui facilitent le transit intestinal. Les fruits, légumes, céréales complètes, légumineuses, noix et graines sont de bonnes sources.

**Impact** – Manger assez de fibres aide à garder un intestin sain, soutient la digestion et réduit les risques de maladies comme le diabète, l’obésité et certains cancers comme le cancer colorectal. Beaucoup d’adultes ne consomment pas assez de fibres, donc ces informations peuvent aider à mieux choisir ses aliments. Les compléments de fibres peuvent aussi aider, mais il faut augmenter la consommation doucement pour éviter des troubles digestifs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les gens peuvent utiliser des outils comme le calculateur des apports nutritionnels recommandés du USDA pour connaître leurs besoins en fibres. Ajouter plus d’aliments riches en fibres ou utiliser des compléments peut aider à atteindre ces objectifs. Les chercheurs continueront à étudier l’effet des fibres sur la santé, ce qui pourrait mener à de nouveaux conseils pour prévenir les maladies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Manger la bonne quantité de fibres chaque jour soutient la santé intestinale et réduit le risque de maladies graves comme le diabète et certains cancers.
articles.personality_title_es: Comer más fibra diariamente mejora la salud intestinal y reduce riesgos de enfermedades
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Comer suficiente fibra es importante para una buena digestión y salud general. La fibra alimenta las bacterias buenas del intestino y puede reducir el riesgo de obesidad, diabetes y algunos tipos de cáncer. Recientemente, la tendencia llamada “fibermaxxing” anima a las personas a consumir la cantidad recomendada de fibra todos los días.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos como Jennifer Lee de la Universidad de Tufts explican que el fibermaxxing significa comer suficiente fibra según el peso corporal. Los adultos deben consumir entre 22 y 34 gramos de fibra al día, según la edad y el sexo. La fibra se divide en dos tipos: soluble, que se disuelve en agua y ayuda a controlar el azúcar y el colesterol, e insoluble, que ayuda a mover los desechos en el sistema digestivo. Frutas, verduras, granos enteros, legumbres, nueces y semillas son buenas fuentes.

**Impacto** – Comer suficiente fibra ayuda a mantener un intestino sano, apoya la digestión y reduce el riesgo de enfermedades como la diabetes, la obesidad y ciertos cánceres como el colorrectal. Muchas personas no consumen suficiente fibra, por lo que esta información puede ayudar a elegir mejores alimentos. Los suplementos de fibra también pueden ayudar, pero es importante aumentar la fibra poco a poco para evitar problemas estomacales.

**Próximo paso** – Las personas pueden usar herramientas como la calculadora de Ingestas Dietéticas de Referencia del USDA para saber cuánta fibra necesitan. Añadir más alimentos ricos en fibra o usar suplementos puede ayudar a alcanzar estas metas. Los científicos seguirán estudiando cómo la fibra afecta la salud, lo que podría llevar a nuevos consejos para prevenir enfermedades.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Comer la cantidad correcta de fibra cada día ayuda a la salud intestinal y reduce el riesgo de enfermedades graves como la diabetes y ciertos cánceres.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant, stylized human digestive tract formed from intertwined colorful fiber-rich foods—such as apples, broccoli, whole grains, legumes, and seeds—flowing smoothly like a river, symbolizing balanced gut health and the nurturing power of dietary fiber.

articles.title: Parents’ stress may be quietly driving childhood obesity, Yale study finds
articles.summary: A Yale study found that lowering parent stress can help protect young children from obesity. When parents practiced mindfulness and stress-management skills, their kids showed healthier eating patterns and avoided the weight gain seen in families that only focused on diet and exercise.
articles.content:
Childhood obesity has been increasing in recent years. According to the U.S. Centers for Disease Control, about one in five children and teenagers in the United States met the clinical definition of obesity in 2024.

Preventing obesity in children is not simple. For many years, the main approaches have focused on encouraging healthy eating and regular physical activity. Researchers at Yale now suggest that another important factor should be added to that list: reducing stress in parents.

A research team led by Yale psychologist Rajita Sinha found evidence that lowering parental stress may help reduce the risk of obesity in young children.

"It's the third leg of the stool," said Sinha. "We already knew that stress can be a big contributor in the development of childhood obesity. The surprise was that when parents handled stress better, their parenting improved, and their young child's obesity risk went down."

The findings were published in the journal Pediatrics.

Parent Stress May Influence Children's Eating and Health

Earlier studies have shown that children are more likely to develop obesity if their parents are obese. Researchers have also suspected that parental stress may be another hidden contributor to obesity in early childhood.

Previous work has shown that stressed parents are more likely to depend on fast food and less healthy eating habits. These choices can influence children's behavior and food preferences. When parents feel overwhelmed, family routines can break down, unhealthy food choices may become more common, and positive parenting behaviors can decline.

Still, most current childhood obesity prevention programs focus mainly on nutrition education and physical activity. According to Sinha, these efforts often fail to create lasting improvements.

Sinha is the Foundations Fund Professor in Psychiatry and a professor in neuroscience and child study at Yale School of Medicine.

Testing a Stress Reduction Program for Parents

To explore the role of parental stress, researchers conducted a 12 week randomized prevention trial involving 114 parents from diverse ethnic and socioeconomic backgrounds. All participants had children between two and five years old who were overweight or obese.

Parents were assigned to one of two groups. One group participated in a stress focused program called Parenting Mindfully for Health (PMH). This program taught mindfulness techniques and behavioral self regulation skills while also providing guidance on healthy nutrition and physical activity.

The other group served as a comparison group and received only counseling about nutrition and physical activity.

Both groups met once a week for sessions lasting up to two hours. During the 12 week program, researchers measured parent stress levels and tracked the children's weight. Children's weight was also measured three months after the program ended.

Researchers also monitored parenting behaviors such as warmth, listening, patience, and positive emotional interactions, along with children's healthy and unhealthy food intake before and after the intervention.

Results Show Improvements When Parent Stress Drops

By the end of the study, only the PMH group experienced lower levels of parent stress, improved parenting behaviors, and a reduction in unhealthy eating among their children. Importantly, the children in this group did not show significant weight gain three months after the program ended.

The control group showed a different pattern. Parents in that group did not experience improvements in stress levels, parenting behaviors, or children's unhealthy food intake. Their children gained significantly more weight and were six times more likely to move into the overweight or obesity risk category at the three month follow up.

Researchers also observed that the link between high parent stress, weaker parenting behaviors, and lower healthy food intake in children remained in the control group after three months. In contrast, this connection was no longer significant in the PMH group.

"The combination of mindfulness with behavioral self-regulation to manage stress, integrated with healthy nutrition and physical activity, seemed to protect the young children from some of the negative effects of stress on weight gain," Sinha said.

Building on Research Into Stress and Health

This work builds on ongoing research at the Yale Stress Center. The center is an interdisciplinary consortium created with support from a 2007 National Institutes of Health Common Fund initiative that studies the biology of stress, health behaviors, and their effects on chronic mental and physical illness.

"Childhood obesity is such a major issue right now, and the results of this study are highly relevant to the current administration's priority of reducing childhood chronic diseases," said Sinha. "When people start moving up the weight scale, their risk of obesity-related illnesses, even in children, is increased."

The findings suggest that long term studies of Parenting Mindfully for Health could provide further insight into reducing childhood obesity risk. According to Sinha, results from a larger group of families followed for two years are expected in the future.

Research Team and Support

The study was co led by Wendy Silverman, the Alfred A. Professor in the Child Study Center and professor of psychology, and Ania Jastreboff, the Harvey and Kate Cushing Professor of Medicine and professor of pediatrics. Additional authors came from the Yale School of Medicine departments of pediatrics and neuroscience and the Yale Child Study Center.

Researchers from the Bethesda Group, the Chicago School of Professional Psychology, the University of New Mexico, and George Mason University also contributed to the study.

The research was supported by the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases (NIDDK).
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a Yale study demonstrating that reducing parental stress through mindfulness and stress-management significantly helps prevent childhood obesity, a major public health issue. The study involved a diverse group of families, showed measurable benefits in children's eating habits and weight outcomes, and suggests a new, effective approach to tackling childhood obesity beyond diet and exercise. This represents a meaningful, evidence-based advancement with broad societal implications for child health.
articles.personality_title: Reducing parent stress helps prevent childhood obesity, Yale study shows
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Childhood obesity is a growing problem in the United States, affecting about one in five children and teens. Traditionally, efforts to prevent obesity have focused on healthy eating and exercise. However, researchers at Yale University wanted to explore if lowering stress in parents could also help protect children from gaining too much weight.

**What happened** – Yale scientists led by psychologist Rajita Sinha conducted a 12-week study with 114 parents of young children who were overweight or obese. The parents were split into two groups: one learned mindfulness and stress-management skills along with advice on nutrition and exercise, while the other group received only nutrition and exercise counseling. The study tracked parent stress levels, parenting behaviors, children's eating habits, and weight over the program and three months afterward.

**Impact** – The group that practiced stress reduction showed lower parent stress, better parenting behaviors like patience and warmth, and healthier eating in their children. These children did not gain significant weight after the program ended. In contrast, children in the group without stress support gained more weight and were six times more likely to become overweight or obese. This shows that reducing parent stress can directly influence a child's risk of obesity, adding an important new piece to childhood health strategies.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to follow a larger group of families for two years to see if these benefits last over time. This could lead to wider use of stress-reduction programs for parents as part of efforts to prevent childhood obesity.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Teaching parents to manage stress along with healthy habits helps protect young children from obesity more effectively than focusing on diet and exercise alone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Réduire le stress des parents aide à prévenir l'obésité infantile, révèle une étude de Yale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'obésité chez les enfants est un problème croissant aux États-Unis, touchant environ un enfant ou adolescent sur cinq. Jusqu'à présent, la prévention se concentrait surtout sur une alimentation saine et l'exercice. Mais des chercheurs de Yale ont voulu vérifier si réduire le stress des parents pouvait aussi protéger les enfants contre la prise de poids.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe menée par la psychologue Rajita Sinha a mené une étude de 12 semaines avec 114 parents d'enfants en surpoids ou obèses. Les parents étaient divisés en deux groupes : un groupe a appris la pleine conscience et la gestion du stress, en plus de conseils sur la nutrition et l'exercice, tandis que l'autre groupe n'a reçu que des conseils sur la nutrition et l'exercice. Les chercheurs ont suivi le stress des parents, leur comportement, les habitudes alimentaires et le poids des enfants pendant et après le programme.

**Impact** – Le groupe pratiquant la réduction du stress a montré une baisse du stress parental, de meilleurs comportements comme la patience et la chaleur, et des enfants qui mangeaient plus sainement. Ces enfants n'ont pas pris de poids significatif après le programme. À l'inverse, les enfants du groupe sans soutien contre le stress ont pris plus de poids et étaient six fois plus susceptibles de devenir en surpoids ou obèses. Cela montre que réduire le stress des parents influence directement le risque d'obésité chez l'enfant.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de suivre un plus grand nombre de familles pendant deux ans pour vérifier si ces effets durent dans le temps. Cela pourrait encourager l'utilisation plus large de programmes de gestion du stress pour les parents dans la prévention de l'obésité infantile.

**En résumé** – Apprendre aux parents à gérer leur stress, en plus d’adopter de bonnes habitudes, protège mieux les jeunes enfants contre l’obésité que de se concentrer uniquement sur l’alimentation et l’exercice.
articles.personality_title_es: Reducir el estrés de los padres ayuda a prevenir la obesidad infantil, revela estudio de Yale
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La obesidad infantil está aumentando en Estados Unidos, afectando a uno de cada cinco niños y adolescentes. Hasta ahora, los esfuerzos para prevenirla se han centrado en la alimentación saludable y el ejercicio. Sin embargo, investigadores de Yale quisieron explorar si reducir el estrés en los padres también podría ayudar a proteger a los niños del aumento de peso.

**Qué sucedió** – Un equipo liderado por la psicóloga Rajita Sinha realizó un estudio de 12 semanas con 114 padres de niños con sobrepeso u obesidad. Los padres se dividieron en dos grupos: uno aprendió técnicas de atención plena y manejo del estrés junto con consejos sobre nutrición y ejercicio, y el otro grupo solo recibió consejos sobre nutrición y ejercicio. El estudio midió el estrés parental, comportamientos de crianza, hábitos alimenticios y peso de los niños durante y después del programa.

**Impacto** – El grupo que practicó la reducción del estrés mostró menos estrés parental, mejores comportamientos como paciencia y calidez, y niños con hábitos alimenticios más saludables. Estos niños no ganaron peso significativo tras el programa. En cambio, los niños del grupo sin apoyo para el estrés aumentaron más de peso y tenían seis veces más probabilidades de volverse obesos o con sobrepeso. Esto muestra que reducir el estrés de los padres influye directamente en el riesgo de obesidad infantil.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir a un grupo más grande de familias durante dos años para ver si estos beneficios se mantienen. Esto podría llevar al uso más amplio de programas para reducir el estrés parental como parte de la prevención de la obesidad infantil.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Enseñar a los padres a controlar el estrés junto con hábitos saludables protege mejor a los niños pequeños de la obesidad que enfocarse solo en la dieta y el ejercicio.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a nurturing parent figure gently cradling a young child-shaped figure made of soft, rounded shapes, surrounded by calming, flowing ribbons symbolizing mindfulness and stress relief, with healthy fruits and playful toys subtly integrated into the scene, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: Monthly HIV-drug injections offer potent alternative to daily tablets
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00669-8Long-lasting treatment suppresses HIV in people with mental illness and other conditions that make it challenging to adhere to standard treatments.
articles.content: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant advancement in HIV treatment with monthly drug injections that provide a potent alternative to daily tablets. This long-lasting treatment improves adherence for people with mental illness and other conditions, offering a meaningful health benefit to a broad population affected by HIV.
articles.personality_title: Monthly HIV injections improve treatment for people struggling with daily pills
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – People living with HIV usually take daily pills to keep the virus under control. However, some find it hard to take these pills every day, especially those with mental illnesses or other health challenges.

**What happened** – Scientists have developed a new treatment that uses monthly injections instead of daily tablets. These injections release medicine slowly over time, helping to control HIV for a whole month with just one shot.

**Impact** – This new approach makes it easier for people who struggle with daily medication routines to stay healthy. It can reduce missed doses and help more people keep the virus under control, which is important for their well-being and to prevent spreading HIV.

**What's next step** – Doctors will start offering these monthly injections to more patients, especially those who have trouble taking pills every day. Researchers will also continue studying this treatment to make it even better and available worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Monthly HIV drug injections offer a simpler and effective way for people with challenges to manage their treatment and stay healthy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des injections mensuelles contre le VIH facilitent le traitement pour ceux qui ont du mal avec les pilules quotidiennes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les personnes vivant avec le VIH prennent généralement des pilules tous les jours pour contrôler le virus. Mais certains ont du mal à suivre ce traitement quotidien, notamment ceux qui ont des troubles mentaux ou d’autres problèmes de santé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont mis au point un nouveau traitement sous forme d’injections mensuelles. Ces injections libèrent lentement le médicament, permettant de contrôler le VIH pendant un mois avec une seule injection.

**Impact** – Cette méthode facilite la vie des personnes qui ont des difficultés à prendre des pilules chaque jour. Elle réduit les risques d’oublis et aide plus de patients à garder le virus sous contrôle, ce qui est important pour leur santé et pour éviter la transmission.

**Prochaine étape** – Les médecins proposeront ces injections mensuelles à davantage de patients, surtout à ceux qui ont du mal avec les pilules quotidiennes. Les chercheurs continueront aussi à améliorer ce traitement pour qu’il soit disponible partout.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les injections mensuelles contre le VIH offrent une solution plus simple et efficace pour aider les personnes en difficulté à suivre leur traitement et rester en bonne santé.
articles.personality_title_es: Inyecciones mensuales contra el VIH facilitan el tratamiento para quienes tienen dificultades con las pastillas diarias
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las personas con VIH suelen tomar pastillas diarias para controlar el virus. Pero algunos tienen problemas para seguir este tratamiento, especialmente quienes tienen enfermedades mentales u otras condiciones de salud.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron un nuevo tratamiento con inyecciones mensuales. Estas inyecciones liberan el medicamento lentamente, controlando el VIH durante un mes con una sola aplicación.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva opción facilita la vida de quienes tienen dificultades para tomar pastillas todos los días. Reduce los olvidos y ayuda a más personas a mantener el virus controlado, lo cual es importante para su salud y para evitar la transmisión.

**Próximo paso** – Los médicos comenzarán a ofrecer estas inyecciones mensuales a más pacientes, especialmente a quienes tienen problemas con las pastillas diarias. Los investigadores seguirán estudiando para mejorar el tratamiento y hacerlo accesible en todo el mundo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las inyecciones mensuales contra el VIH ofrecen una forma más sencilla y efectiva para que las personas con dificultades manejen su tratamiento y se mantengan saludables.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a pair of intertwined hands gently holding a sleek, glowing syringe and a small, round pill side by side, set against a soft, natural-toned background symbolizing care and medical progress.
